questions.q: What is a sprint in Scrum methodology?
options: A rapid debugging session
A fixed time-box (typically 1-4 weeks) in which a team completes a set amount of work
An emergency meeting called to discuss failures
A performance optimization technique
questions.explanation: A sprint is a fixed iteration in Scrum (usually 2 weeks) where a team commits to completing specific work items. Sprints provide structure, predictability, and regular reflection. Option 0 is debugging, option 2 is an unplanned meeting, and option 3 is a technical practice.

questions.q: Which of these is a core principle of Agile methodology?
options: Complete all documentation before writing code
Responding to change over following a rigid plan
Avoiding all direct communication in favor of written documentation
Testing only at the end of development
questions.explanation: Agile values responding to change and customer feedback over following a fixed plan. This flexibility enables teams to adapt to new requirements. Options 0, 2, and 3 represent waterfall or outdated practices that Agile explicitly rejects.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Kanban board in workflow management?
options: To record employee vacation schedules
To visualize work items, their status, and workflow to limit work in progress and improve flow
To track only completed tasks
To replace all written communication
questions.explanation: Kanban boards visualize workflow with columns representing stages (To Do, In Progress, Done), helping teams see bottlenecks and manage work-in-progress limits. Option 0 is a calendar function, option 2 ignores incomplete work, and option 3 is false.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between Scrum and Kanban?
options: Scrum uses sprints and iterations, while Kanban emphasizes continuous flow with no fixed iterations
Kanban is more expensive than Scrum
Scrum works only for small teams
Kanban requires more documentation than Scrum
questions.explanation: Scrum uses time-boxed sprints with defined roles and ceremonies, while Kanban focuses on continuous delivery with work-in-progress limits and no sprints. Both are Agile but have different structures. Options 1, 2, and 3 are misconceptions about these methodologies.

questions.q: What does CI/CD stand for and why is it valuable?
options: Code Integration/Code Division, used to split teams
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment, enabling automated testing and rapid, reliable releases
Client Interface/Content Database, for website management
Compiler Integration/Configuration Distribution, for build systems
questions.explanation: CI/CD automates testing and deployment, enabling frequent, reliable releases with rapid feedback. Continuous Integration tests each commit, Continuous Deployment automates releases. Options 0, 2, and 3 are incorrect definitions that don't relate to software delivery.

questions.q: How does Waterfall methodology differ fundamentally from Agile?
options: Waterfall is faster than Agile
Waterfall completes each phase sequentially with minimal overlap, while Agile works iteratively with frequent feedback
Waterfall requires fewer developers
Agile never involves testing
questions.explanation: Waterfall follows a linear, phase-by-phase approach (requirements, design, development, testing, deployment), while Agile iterates in cycles with continuous feedback. Waterfall is less flexible but provides clear upfront specifications. Options 0, 2, and 3 are misconceptions.

questions.q: What is the role of a Product Owner in Scrum?
options: To write all the code for the product
To manage the product backlog, prioritize features, and communicate with stakeholders
To conduct all code reviews
To approve all pull requests before deployment
questions.explanation: The Product Owner manages the backlog, prioritizes user stories based on business value, and communicates customer needs to the development team. This role ensures the team builds the right product. Options 0, 2, and 3 are technical responsibilities of developers and engineers.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a retrospective meeting in Scrum?
options: To blame team members for failures
To plan the next sprint's technical architecture
To reflect on what went well, what didn't, and how to improve in future sprints
To evaluate individual developer performance
questions.explanation: Retrospectives encourage team reflection and continuous improvement, discussing processes, not individuals. This blameless approach builds trust and enables iterative process enhancements. Options 0, 1, and 3 misunderstand the meeting's constructive purpose.

questions.q: What is a user story in Agile development?
options: A detailed technical specification for developers
A narrative description of a feature from the user's perspective to prompt conversation and planning
A bug report written by customers
A historical record of all code changes
questions.explanation: User stories describe features in user-centric language (As a user, I want X so that Y), fostering shared understanding and conversation. They're less formal than specifications. Option 0 describes technical specs, option 2 is bug reporting, and option 3 is version control history.

questions.q: Why is frequent communication emphasized in Agile teams?
options: To distract developers from coding
Because face-to-face interaction reduces misunderstandings and enables quick problem-solving
To comply with government regulations
Because it eliminates the need for code reviews
questions.explanation: Direct communication minimizes ambiguity, accelerates decision-making, and catches issues early. This is valued over extensive documentation in Agile. Options 0, 2, and 3 misrepresent Agile principles or are false.
